content:
The rooftop was quiet, suspended in that delicate hush only sunrise could grant. The sun hadn’t quite crested the horizon, but its soft gold already stretched over the rooftops and settled against Auri’s shoulders. She moved along the peak with practiced balance, a basket in one hand and a folded blanket under the other. The morning wind tugged at her dark hair as she crossed to the little platform where she’d told Vornakir to meet her.

He had insisted on planning his own birthday party, and as much as she knew he would love that, Auri wanted him to have something else, too. 

She spread the blanket, then set the basket in one corner. A cluster of folded paper flowers she’d made herself served as a centerpiece. The waiting gave her mind room to wander.

Auri first noticed Vornakir because he was one of the few students who not only kept pace with her, but often stepped ahead. Their rivalry had become a mix of playful banter and helped push her forward, a rhythm she relied on. 

And then, one day, he didn’t show up to class. 

She heard that he was injured, but nothing more. 
She kept working– and even began completing his assignments– not out of pity, but because she refused to win by default. Respect drove her more fiercely than competition ever had. 

Later, he returned. Without an arm. She remembered the hush that fell over the room, how he walked with rigid steadiness, meeting every stare directly. Something shifted in her then, quietly and permanently.

After that, she allowed herself to know him, and what she found was someone loyal, fiercely protective, and never as effortless as he appeared. Their banter softened into something warmer.

Auri arranged the snacks she’d brought– fruit, bread, a few savory bites, and the cookies one of the tower’s ghosts had taught her to make. It wasn’t anything grand, but it was hers. Her effort, her care, and her intention to do something for him. 

Once she was finished, Auri opened her notes and began to read to pass the time. And there she stayed, surrounded by sunlight and paper flowers, saving this moment for him. <@206175579358232578>

It was quite interesting, the stark difference his missing arm prompted at first yet the way he carried himself was beyond commendable. Deep down, there was a twisting agony and gnawing anger at his loss but on the surface, none could discern the turmoil within. His chin was still raised, his shoulders still square and his gait still regal as if every room he walked into belonged to him. 

When he learned she had kept up with his classwork for him, that’s when she first got a peek at the real him, and it revealed just how attentive he was. It arrived outside her room, whether before or after she moved into the dorm; a small package with a note on it. In elegant golden lettering, the words simply said: *“You never make enough time for proper meals. I have prepared for you one week of meals that you can easily eat during study or between tasks on your schedule in place of your snacks to repay the week of work you did for me. Please take care of yourself more, the world and I both appreciate people that put in the level of work you do. — Vornakir Vanori”*

Inside the parcel was seven small tubs sealed and fresh for her usage. Special made salads, containing silvered almonds, bacon, romaine hearts, grape tomatoes, Swiss and Parmesan cheese, croutons, light dousing of olive oil, garlic, Dijon mustard, honey and topped with salt and pepper. Salad de Vanori, a fragrant recipe served in the Vanori manor often for those desiring a salad she’d come to know. All prepped for easy consumption.

He arrived almost exactly on time, keeping in with her strict schedule, dressed more casually than the later party would have him presented. But still he made the effort to be presentable with groomed hair, and well pressed clothes as always in public. He bowed his head toward her, “Good morning, Miss Auri. How long do I have the pleasure of your company this morning?” He asked perhaps in regard to her schedule as to respect her time.

As he approached, Auri felt something in her chest settle.

She hadn’t let anyone see how difficult that first term away from the tower had been. While the rest of the students whispered and joked about her living off the library and surviving via photosynthesis, what they didn’t know was that she didn’t skip meals just because she was busy or didn’t need to eat. 

That first week he was absent, when she’d taken on double the workload to keep their rivalry fair, she’d barely slept. She remembered sitting at her desk late one night, stomach twisting from anxiety, telling herself she could do it anyway. That her determination and will alone could sustain her. But the truth was, she was drowning.

Then, a package arrived at her door. And it wasn’t the elegant lettering, presentation, or the polite returning of the favor that made her heart ache, but the fact that someone *noticed*. That someone saw her pushing herself hard enough to break and cared enough to stop her. 

She’d wiped her face quickly, muttering at herself to get it together for god’s sake, crying over a salad, but she’d eaten the whole thing. 
Ever since then, she made an effort to eat more often. To take better care of herself. Even if she still had a long way to go in that department.

So today…what looked like small, simple gestures were a way of letting him feel what he once gave her. That feeling of being appreciated and cared for. 

Auri stood to greet him, amazed at how casual for him still somehow meant better put-together than half the school. She was dressed in a black, cropped shirt and checkered pants, her asymmetrically cut hair half pulled back with a silver clip. Not fancy by any means, but unmistakably herself.

“Good morning, Vornakir. Or should I say…*Mr.* Vornakir? Are we using honorifics with each other now?” she teased with an easy smile. “At least you didn’t use my full name. You know I’m not a fan.”

When he asked her about her schedule, Auri pretended to check an invisible planner. “About two hours. I have a study group later, and then your big, birthday production.” 

She gestured to the blanket and snacks she’d prepared and patted a spot beside her. “But this part of your day? This one is just for us.”

Something both of them must know by now, if for only paying attention to one another, is that the pair of them had this one major flaw in common. To be the best, to reach their goal, nothing could stand in their way. They’d push and push and push until they’d reach a state of pure burn out if they must. 

For her most recently he’d noted it took form of starving herself and worrying herself until she was one foot in the grave. For him, it was erupting on a training field alone and sending every last drop of arcana in his veins out into the world in the form of dozens of spells. 

It took someone of the same caliber to know how they felt, about how they functioned and worked. When he had learned she wanted to make sure his efforts weren’t sent to waste because of his accident, he couldn’t imagine simply noticing her effort and making her food would mean so much. 

To him, it wasn’t nearly enough to repay her for what she’d done for him. But it was a start. And the result of that was what he saw before him. Someone who saw a part of him most others just looked over. 

He was dressed this morning in a well crafted long sleeve shirt with the left arm masterfully altered into removal, stitched closed as if it had never been there. Over his left shoulder, a simple yet elegant dueling cloak he wore often in his matches - but now it’s most important purpose was to block where the arm used to be, to make people less uncomfortable. The shirt was black with trimmings of silver while the vest was black with filigree of gold. 

“Mister Vornakir? As much as I adore honorifics, and as important as they tend to be in my position, I don’t require any. I simply called you ‘Miss Auri’ to respect the work you put in for me today.” He said, gesturing with his hand toward the spread. 

“Two whole hours for us? I hope my company makes it worth the while.” He said with a polite incline of his head and a charismatic smile fit for his position. 

He gestured once more to the spread and then offered a hand to her as if to help her lower herself to sit as a gentleman ought.

Auri let a slight smile curl at her lips. “I appreciate the formality, but just Auri is fine,” she said gently, her tail flicking once in amused punctuation.

“And yes, two hours just for you.” Her voice dipped into a teasing lilt. “It *is* your birthday, after all. How could I possibly make the day all about you in less than two hours before your big party? Admit it– you wouldn’t be satisfied with anything less than that.”

Her hand slipped into his, and she lowered herself onto the blanket with effortless grace, dark hair spilling over her shoulder like ink. “Your company, by the way,” she added softly, “is always worth it. Don’t ever doubt that. Not even in jest.”

She reached into the basket and pulled out one of the small, hand-painted plates adorned with tiny green leaves. As she set it before him, her tail curled loosely around her– a sign she was thinking, weighing something carefully. Her amber gaze found his cape again, draped over his shoulder like a quiet shadow.

“You know…” she began, her voice warm but quieter now, “you don’t need to hide around me.”

Her fingers brushed the rim of the wine glass she held, then paused. She lifted her eyes to his, searching, making absolutely sure that he could stop her at any moment. Only then did she let her hand drift toward the clasp of his cape. 

“I remember what you told me,” she said. The words were soft, hesitant only out of respect. “About what you went through.”

If allowed, her fingers rested lightly on the fabric, waiting for the smallest signal before easing the cape from his shoulder. If he gave any indication he did not want her to remove it, she would withdraw immediately and without judgment. But if he allowed it, she gathered the cloth with a quiet reverence, folding it neatly at his side as though handling something meaningful. Not fragile, but important.

Her eyes flicked to the place where his arm once was. Not lingering enough to make it a wound, but long enough to show she *saw* him fully, before facing him.

“This isn’t anything to hide. Not from me. Not from anyone.”

With that, she settled back to her place on the blanket, reclaiming the wine glass she’d set aside. She cleared her throat lightly, a small, self-aware smile tugging at her mouth as she offered him the glass.

Before he took her hand, he raised his to his chest in both faux surprise and playful jest in turn. “Careful, you’ll inflate my ego like that.” He says, as if there was room to inflate it more than it typically was. 

Regardless he kept his manners and took her hand to help her down to sit before he lowered himself as well - sitting somewhat angled to half face her at her side. 

As was customary, he waved his hand to the side and in a shower of golden sparks his mage hand blinked into reality to assist him as he began to pull his hair up into a somewhat messy half ponytail, to keep it out of his face.

Something she may know or may have learned in her time around him was that it was custom for his people to always present themselves well groomed and taken care of - a picture of elegance. To see him in any manner of casual or messy often denoted a sense of trust. 

Even more trust was present if he allowed someone to touch him without fear, or be on his left side as she was now. Where he was weakest. “You flatter me, Auri. But nonetheless it’s appreciated. For what it’s worth, your company is always welcome and worth it as well. Not just because of friendship but your efforts make me strive to be better.” 

As he turned his face to rest soft, molten gold eyes on her his lips quirked into a smile that was real. “Competition breeds excellence, and having someone capable and intelligent around me only bolsters my own strength. Though I appreciate the effort I see you put into everything beyond what it does for me.” He explained, hoping he didn’t give the wrong impression. He was still getting used to this ‘keeping friendships’ thing.

In truth, it was watching her that inspired him to keep pushing on after his incident. The way setbacks frustrated her but never held her down, he admired it deeply. She was a lot like him, and that made his desire to be closer to her that much easier to grapple with. He too often felt like he was surrounded by people who didn’t have enough drive. 

Watching her carefully, his auric stare shifted to the basket as she produced the plate. He was always cautious when people made him food, or drinks or anything of the sort. Poison wasn’t an uncommon tactic to rid this world of his mortality. But watching her body language led to watching her tail, and his head quirked to the side.

Before he could ask what was troubling her, she spoke. He was more cautious now as she wanted to reach forward. For the first couple of moments he hesitated, not leaning closer but not leaning away. Just letting his eyes drill holes through her hands. 

It took a couple extra lengthy moments before he’d give a single, curt, short nod to allow her to remove it. And as she did, a small touch of warmth painted his cheeks while she spoke. 

“You know, I was enamored with your presentation at Sponsorship Day. It really encapsulated who you are at your core. I mention it, not just to praise you but so maybe you’ll understand what I mean when I say this.” He began, pausing to reach out and take the glass she offered him. 

Once he settled back to his position sitting properly, he met her gaze and held it. “You perplex me. Not in a bad way, I’m just not used to people caring the way you do. Care may not even be the correct word for it; I’ve experienced that but you do it in a manner I’m not used to. You didn’t…pity me when I lost my arm. You just wanted to help, maybe out of respect. Maybe just because it’s who you are.” he continued, temporarily handing off his glass so he could reach up and rub the back of his neck. After taking the glass back, he spoke further.

“I guess I’m just trying to say thank you for caring. And after seeing what happened with Sol, I…think I just want to make sure you know your efforts are seen. What you do for people, what you strive for in yourself. You’re seen.” He finished on that, giving another nod firmly, a couple strands of black hair falling out of place along with the less common golden strands.

Auri’s breath caught. Being seen– *truly* seen– struck deeper than she was prepared for. 

She looked down for a moment, giving herself the heartbeat she needed to smooth her expression. 

“Vornakir…” Her voice came out quieter than she intended. She paused, inhaled softly, then tried again. “You say I perplex you, but you…surprise me. In the best way.” 

A small, earnest smile tugged at her lips.

“I helped you because I know what it looks like when someone is trying so hard not to fall apart that they forget they’re allowed to lean on someone.” She let out a soft breath. “And you reminded me of that, too. I’ve spent my whole life working toward one goal in a place full of people who were looking after me. Then, when I came here, I was so focused on my work that I forgot to look after myself.”

Her eyes softened. “When you gave me the meals you made…it helped remind me to take care of myself. I was under so much pressure that I could barely eat. But you helped me remember. And that…meant a lot to me.” 

Her amber gaze lifted– briefly, tellingly– to all the rules he’d broken this morning. He let her touch him, let her cook for him, let himself sit unguarded beside her. Each one of those meant trust. She knew exactly how much that mattered.

She let herself lean back on her palms, letting the morning light warm the edges of her thoughts. “And just so we’re clear,” she added with quiet conviction, “You don’t have to thank me for seeing you,” she said, her voice dropping a bit as she angled herself a little closer– subtle, intentional, but not overwhelming. “I was already looking.”

A breath. A beat. The faint widening of her eyes at how honest she’d become without meaning to at the crack of dawn. Her cheeks flushed, just slightly. To save herself from it, she straightened abruptly, reached for a cookie, and offered it toward him like a truce flag. 

“Compliments, emotional vulnerability, snacks, and a gift I haven’t even given you yet…” She poured them each a glass of wine and lifted hers toward him to toast. “Hopefully you’re starting to feel properly celebrated by now.”

Vornakir observed her carefully. He could see, just like himself, her own composure slipped some. 

That was a nice touch, to not be the only one to let their guard down. It made it so much easier for him to show these changes in his personality. For her to get to see the real him like so few people got to do. 

One of his ears twitched, the piercing that chained from the tip of the ear down to the lobe clattering against itself. He hadn't noticed that he leaned closer a little as she spoke, enamored with the way she spoke. He had a weakness for people that spoke directly like this and a profound respect. In a society that craves deception and rewards backstabbing, someone who was honest and upfront won a lot of points with him. 

His gaze didn't lower but his brows did as his expression softened. "Truly, I think losing my arm was more eye opening to me than almost anything else. Seeing death firsthand, literally and figuratively," He continued as he recalled the Guardian of the Afterlife he spoke with briefly. "It really put a lot of things into perspective. Especially at how frail life really is." 

That's when he gestured toward her with his fingers outward and palm up. "Such as seeing someone drowning at the same time I was burning out. Sometimes you need someone to pull you out of the water, or put out the fire. Someone to make you food when you're too overwhelmed to do it, or someone to force you to rest when you refuse to stop." 

The way she leaned in went somewhat unnoticed. He was still closer himself, not just some of the rules he'd broken of his own but *most* of them. His guard was non-existent, his attention wasn't on his surroundings or potential attackers. Her every move wasn't scrutinized, he didn't anticipate her to bring him harm. Every move closer, he unwittingly matched her inch by inch. It was a natural draw. 

Her words brought him back to reality, the stained glass gold seeming to glow with a radiant emotion. He opened his mouth to say something back, the words 'I was already looking' fanning the ember inside. But then she abruptly shifted making him realize just how much they were both leaning in. He straightened up, deliberately slower than her, and cleared his throat while taking the glass back from his mage hand to allow it to be filled. The hand took the cookie instead, and he lifted the glass in return toast. 

"And a gift? If I weren't already a spoiled Prince, you'd do well enough spoiling me all on your own. Especially since your company has already been gift enough." He clinked his glass lightly against hers, taking a generous sip without even bothering to investigate it for poison. He savored the taste, licking his lips and humming in satisfaction. "As far as I'm concerned, the party will pale compared to this." His eyes briefly shifted from her to the beauty of the rising sun washing them in light and warmth.

Auri listened— truly listened— as Vornakir spoke. The quiet way he explained how losing his arm had cracked something open in him, how meeting the Guardian of the Afterlife had shaken his understanding of just how fragile life is, and how, despite his instinct to bear everything alone, sometimes even he needed someone to lean on. Although she had never faced death, Auri knew what it was to take on too much to the point of breaking.

There was a gravity to his words, but also a softness she knew he rarely let anyone see. She realized he was offering that softness to her.

When they both straightened, she became fully aware of how close they had been leaning only a moment ago. How natural it had felt. How her body had eased toward him without thinking. A warmth fluttered in her chest, like the quiet spark before a spell kindles. Had he pulled back from her more slowly than she had from him…or was that simply hope pretending to be memory?

She filled his glass to avoid staring at him too long. “Well, yes,” she said with a half-smile, “It’s your birthday. I’m obligated to spoil you at least a little.” 

They clinked glasses. As she took a sip, she watched as he drank without hesitation. Trusting her magic, her intentions…trusting *her*. She felt the weight of that realization settle. 

The sun was lifting over the ridge now, brushing its warmth across the world in pinks and soft gold. Auri watched along with him, allowing the moment to hold them while she reached into the basket. Her hand found a small shape wrapped in cloth. She hesitated, just briefly, because giving it suddenly felt far more intimate than she had expected. A physical representation of what it was she felt for him. Then she gathered her courage, drew it out, and turned back to him.

A bracelet— charms woven into dark and pale cords, crossing over each other the way paths sometimes do when they are destined to meet. Like everything else today, she had made it herself; each piece chosen with intention, each knot set with care.

She held it toward him in her palm. “For you.” 

Her other hand brushed his shoulder, not to startle him, but to bring his attention fully to her. “Magic is built on three strands,” she said softly. “If one strand is missing, the spell fails to take shape. No matter how strong the caster is.”

She lifted her eyes to his, something quieter and braver settling behind them. “I wanted you to have something that reminds you…asking for help doesn’t weaken the structure. It strengthens it. It keeps everything from unraveling.”

A breath passed between them, soft and unhurried.
 
“You make me better,” she murmured. “You pull me out of the water when I’m drowning. And when the fire comes for you, Vornakir… I’ll pull you out too. Every time.”

Vornakir’s head tilted back, his eyes drawing absent shapes in the clouds as the cresting sun painted the sky in its hues just before the beauty of the sunrise itself offered a hand out. At this angle, she could see the weight under his eyes that he tried to hide so often from everyone. “There was once this man, I can’t for the life of me remember where he came from when he visited our Circle in the Barricade. But I remember he saw me at a low point, when the weight started to crush me. I was younger then. He said something that stuck with me. ‘Somewhere out there, there’s a stranger you’ll never remember but who’ll never forget you’.”

The turn of phrase made him smile as the memory played in his head. His voice was soft as he prattled on, “I never understood it until I met you. I mean no offense when I say, it took until my arm was lost until I recognized you, truly. Until then you were a stranger to me, someone I didn’t even know that wanted to compete with me for marks in class. Yet you still came to my aid, and you still went out of your way to help me.” Briefly he looked out of the corner of his eyes at her. “If we’d never spoken again after that, that phrase would have hit harder. There was a very small window that I almost didn’t want to go on until a pretty Tiefling with high ambitions held out her hand.”

“You didn’t think twice, you just held it out and wanted to lift me up. I carried that kindness with me until I had enough in me to keep going. It never occurred to me that every person I pass is living a life with their own struggles and their own triumphs. A life as mean, and complicated and beautiful as my own. We leave fingerprints on everyone’s hearts and lives that we touch without even realizing it. To you, maybe helping me with homework was insignificant. But seeing the weight on your shoulders and then watching you add more on my behalf was the most thoughtful and incredible thing anyone has ever done for me.”

Silence took him again, and for the next several minutes neither of them spoke. They simply enjoyed the brushstrokes of a radiant sunrise that washed them in its loving embrace. He didn’t intend to tell her how much it meant to him, that someone saw him not as a prince who needed to be taken down a peg but as a man losing his will to live - and giving him an ounce of her own. 

His ear twitched again when she started to move at his side. He patiently waited for her to produce the gift, an amused smirk tugging at his lips as the gentle morning breeze sent a few strands of his ebony and honey streaked hair dancing. 

Delicately he reach out to take the bracelet and turned it this way and that in his fingers, identifying each individual strand but his eyes caught first on the charms to see what they were. What they may symbolize or just simply the shapes of them as he listened to her explanation. 

With her hand on his shoulder, perhaps he didn’t notice or maybe he just didn’t stop himself as he instinctively leaned a little closer with an owlish lilt of his head to the side. His ochre draconic eyes petaled out like flowers, something most people may not notice about his irises after the draconic ascension forced the mild mutation in his already draconic blood - they were settled on her, beholding her with the same reverence he gave the rising sun moments before.

A hushed voice muttered out, afraid to crack the fragility of the moment they shared. “It’ll remind me of you more than anything, but the sentiment isn’t lost on me. And I don’t think I can thank you enough.”

Her next words caused his throat to tighten and he licked his lips tentatively, nervous they were too dry to speak. And then it took a moment to build his courage to speak again though when he finally did his voice remained gentle with her.

“Would you put it on me?” He asked her, knowing good and well he could do so himself with the mage hand. But this felt like a moment that deserved the sentiment of allowing *her* to adorn him with something he’d cherish for life.

Auri didn’t interrupt him once. She let every word settle over her like sunlight spilling into a quiet room, and she noticed the subtle ways he was giving her himself--- the careful cadence of his voice, the way his eyes flicked toward her. Warmth pooled in her chest. *A stranger you'll never remember...who'll never forget you."*

Each sentence he spoke landed like a stone in still water, and she felt the ripples in her own heart. When he spoke of being pushed to the point of breaking, she knew that ache all too well. The nights of relentless pressure, the constant weight of expectations. And when he said she helped him—-***really helped him***, for a moment, the world narrowed to the sound of his voice, the morning light, and the trust blooming between them. 

When the silence reclaimed them, they watched the sunrise unfold together. Gold washed across the horizon, soft and low. She risked a glance at him, noticing the shape of his irises, the breeze stirring his dark hair— and something in her stomach fluttered, low and light. This wasn’t how one looked at a friend. And yet, although she didn’t want to say it yet, she couldn’t stop what stirred within her. She had to look away before he noticed the warmth coloring her face. She wasn’t sure whether he did, but his subtle shift toward her made her wonder if he had, and whether he welcomed it.

Gently, she lifted the bracelet again and shifted closer, her knee brushing his. The contact was small, accidental, yet it hummed through her like a struck string. She didn’t pull back. She didn’t want to. When she took his hand, heat bloomed in her palms. Her thumb brushed over the back of his hand before she could think better of it, a soft slow stroke that felt far too natural.

  “Hold still,” she murmured, trying to sound steady instead of breathless. She fastened the bracelet gently, but her fingers lingered. She liked the way he watched her too, like this wasn’t just a gift, but a moment he was storing away, memorizing her hands on him. Like it might mean as much to him as it did to her.

“From now on…” she whispered, voice dropping without her permission,  “we walk out of the fire together.” Her fingers curled around his.  “And if one of us sinks...the other lifts them back up.”

She hesitated, not in fear, but in the moment of choosing something she wanted. Then she rested her head against his shoulder. The instant their bodies touched, she felt it: that slow, deep pull in her chest. The desire to stay there with him for more than a moment, to lean in just a little more. 

“You let me see you,” she said, not to the sunrise, but to *him*. Her thumb brushed his hand again, soft and instinctive, like she couldn’t help herself. Couldn’t stop the feeling of wanting to be close to him. 

“And I’m glad you did. You don’t have to face anything alone anymore.”

He chanced a glance up at her while she worked on putting the bracelet on him. He studied her face, the curve of her cheeks and the way her lips moved when she spoke or smiled. The lingering heat in her cheeks. The moment meant more to him than he could explain to her, more than he could begin to understand himself. It was foreign, it was alien, it just simply wasn't something he thought he deserved let alone would feel again. 

It slumbered deep within his chest beside the dragon that ruled the caverns in his soul. An emotion locked inside an armoire dumped long forgotten on top of the dragon's hoard of repressed memories, abandoned desire and resignation. The chains rattled with her every touch, the wood splintered with each of her breathless words and the monstrous creature of myth that guarded it lowered his defenses long enough to allow the doors of the armoire to swing wide open by the time she brushed her thumb over his hand. 

She wasn't just seeing the emotional side of him that nobody else got to see, she didn't just get to see the true feelings and softer self he kept behind walls made of obsidian. This morning she watched Lord Vornakir Vanori, Prince of the Obsidian Barricade and Scaleborn Prodigy simply crumble away and turn into a mere nameless man. The throne, the riches, the nobility, the titles, the crown...Even the trial, it all turned to ash behind his auric stare and his heart opened to lay bare before her. 

After the lingering silence, he slowly took his hand back to brush his thumb beneath one of his eyes - Wiping a tear away, just a stray one that he couldn't stop from welling up. He took her hand back and gave it a squeeze, shifting around a little to allow her to do as she wanted. To lay her head on his shoulder. 

The warmth that spread from the moment of contact through him cause that armoire to throb, for the liquid emotion to pour from its doors and seep into the dragon's hoard. He had already spent a chunk of the two hours she had sat aside from him, and he was damned and determined to make the best of every second with her. Even if it meant simply sitting here holding one another watching the colors of the sky shift and the clouds to roll in with their obscure shapes. A brief flash of memory echoed in his mind as his heart yearned to throw those doors wide open to let her see its contents.

> *He lay in his bed, resting in the infirmary alone - nothing new. His friends stopped by now and then, as did his siblings, once the news broke across campus. He was allowed to go to class, but he had to spend much of his free time in the infirmary to rest. He didn't just lose his arm, his entire body was under incredible physical toll. Some days he couldn't even manage classes. Those rare times he did, he held himself with incredible poise. His chin always raised, his chest always full with pride. As if losing his arm made him no less than he was before, as if he were still the King of Campus and every room he walked into. But it was a mask. The same mask he always wore like a second skin. Only one person saw through a crack in it during class the first day he showed up without his arm. A radiant tiefling girl he never took the time to notice before - Nothing new there, he couldn't tell anyone the name of really any classmate he had in this class. She would have noticed the hollow eyes that lost their golden luster, the brilliant shine fading into a matte shell. She would have noticed the way the magelights in class caught his face at the right angle and revealed foundation blended seamlessly under his eyes to hide bags heavier than the weight of expectation on his shoulders. The way his knuckles went white when he heard of how far ahead other students surpassed him in dueling standings during his recovery and how his shoulders slumped anytime he failed at something he used to excel before the accident. It took until the moment she walked into the infirmary for him to truly notice her. And it took until she admitted she'd been keeping up with his work for him while he recovered to know her name. It took until he noticed the weight she carried for him to realize she was just like him. It took until he noticed the weight she lost from not eating to realize she deserved the same kindness she extended to him.*

And it took until today for him to realize she was more than a classmate, more than a friend. The yearning in his chest betrayed that much for him. But he couldn't begin to understand how to process that, not right now. 

A gentle voice that was out of character for him and reserved for her ears murmured out, "Neither do you. Thank you, for today. And everything. If you ever find the spare time in your schedule, let me know. I don't take enough time to admire beauty like this anymore. I would...really like to do it again." 

While she may have been looking at the sky, the sun, the colors and the clouds...

Vornakir was looking at *her*.

Auri didn’t realize Vornakir had been looking at her. She only felt the soft squeeze of his hand, the faint tremor in his breath, the shift when he brushed something from his cheek. She kept her eyes trained on the sky, because if she looked at him now, she wasn’t sure she could hide the way her heart was starting to unravel thread by carefully woven thread. Some truths, she thought, were too new to speak aloud, and this one was too fragile, too important, and too beautiful to risk breaking.

The colors above them bled slowly into one another, pink to coral to the faintest stroke of lavender. She exhaled, as if she could breathe those colors directly into her chest. Soon she would have to return to her schedule, her carefully orchestrated plan. But in this moment, all she wanted to let as to stay right where she was; listening to the tenderness of his voice, feeling how naturally she settled against him, and sensing her heart quietly, irrevocably choosing him, even if she didn't know what that meant. 

“I…I’d really like that too,” she murmured, cheek nestled against his shoulder, the warmth of her dark hair against his jaw. “The day after tomorrow? Same time. Same spot.” A small smile curved her lips. Her fingers played absently with the charms on the bracelet, the cord, the soft fabric of his sleeve. “We could bring something with us. Something that matters. A story, a memory, an object…whatever we want to share.” Her thumb gently brushed over his hand again. “I just think it might be nice…to share more pieces of who we are with each other.”

The Vornakir beside her was different from the one she’d met in class, the one with whom she’d shared an academic, one-sided rivalry for most of the semester. He had always looked so confident, so put-together. But once she began feeling the weight of expectations and responsibility herself, she started to understand that confidence could be a carefully constructed facade, just like hers.

She remembered the moment she saw the truth in him. The strain at the corners of his mouth. The faint, exhausted tremor he tried to bury in a clenched jaw. The mask that had finally begun to crack at its edges. Something in her chest shifted then, like a bead of light breaking free inside a lantern. It surprised her so completely that she spent the next hour convincing herself it was nothing.

But it wasn’t nothing. Not then, and certainly not now.

It was the very beginning of this— whatever this was. This strange, fierce warmth that kept growing in her chest as if her heart had decided, without asking permission, to bloom for him alone. A reckless, wild thing pressing outward, demanding to be felt.

When the last hour came to an end, she reluctantly moved away from him, packing things up with the cookies, the little flowers she’d made, and his cloak that she had folded earlier. 

Should he allow it, she hugged him, and for a breathless heartbeat, the world narrowed to the warmth of her against him, the careful tilt of her horns, the soft thud of her heart she could swear beat so loudly he could feel it against his chest too.

“So,” she whispered against his ear, “I’ll meet you here the day after tomorrow, okay? And don’t forget to bring something you want to share with me…because I’ll have something for you too.”

She pulled back just enough to see him without giving herself away. Her tail swished in a slow, almost shy arc, betraying the tug of her heart even as she tried to stay composed. She didn’t know if she was ready for how quickly it had taken hold, but the thought of pulling away felt impossible. Even when she stepped fully out of his touch, it felt as though some part of her remained pressed against him, refusing to fully leave. An invisible thread impossible to untangle.

Then she let herself go, leaving him with that quiet, lingering pull, a promise that the waiting would be worth it.

And thankfully, she didn’t realize it. Not that he was embarrassed, not that he was shy nor wanted her to be left in the dark. But because now, for as long as he still had breath he would cling to the memory of the shine in her eyes as she drank in the sunrise. He’d have the memory of the way her lips parted and curved upward at the corners, at how radiant that simple smile really was. The memory of the day the sun’s light was outshone.

By her.

His internal canvas was painted in sludges of gray and black with golden lacquer mixed into it to try and outshine the dull thoughtless existence. His biography was countless pages of empty pride and shallow purpose. The spark of life within him was dampened, it had been so many years since he’d truly put brush to canvas or pen to parchment. The beauty of the world was stolen from him, and the smoldering embers of passion doused with reality until now. 

Respect was given to her schedule and her lifestyle, he adored the way she thrived under pressure and was so driven. But it would be the best gift to be the only plan in her schedule. Still, he never wanted to pull her from the plans she had — It would completely shatter who she was, and what drew him to her in the first place. Selfishness wanted him to keep her here all day, but selflessness told him that the sweetest wine was the one you waited to taste until the perfect moment.

When she did speak at last, he quirked a brow and looked back down toward her at the corners of his eyes as to not disturb her position. “It’s a date.” He replied nearly breathless, perhaps a little quicker too than he would have preferred. His mask was slipping more, typically not one to seem so eager. He watched how she played absently with his sleeve and the bracelet. The moment was etched into his memory just as clearly as her smile. 

“I’d be happy with that and I look forward to it. It’d surely be a highlight of my week, next to today.” He said in a playful lift of the tone, the mask further slipping from his face. Each passing day, and moment they shared she’d learn more and more of the real man beneath it. 

For some moments, he had to wonder to himself if he deserved this. If he was allowed to feel that very same blossom of emotion that begged to weave its way into their tapestry. If he was permitted to tie a knot in that thread between them around his finger so the memory of her was never far from his grasp. 

The flicking of her tail, the sudden way she held him and almost didn’t want to let go. And how much she looked forward to their next day together…all of that washed away the doubt and concern like a divine rain. Could she hear the thumping of his heart? See the light flush of his face beneath the kaleidoscope of the sun’s colors? “I’ll have something for you. Something special, I promise.” He assured her, and he was just as reluctant to release her, and when she stepped away he ached. 

An emptiness he never noticed was filled while she was there, and it returned when she wasn’t. While he watched her leave, the plan already began to form. He would leave the rooftop, only for a short time — returning swiftly before the sun could finish its ascent into the sky. He flicked his wrist to summon illusory images of the scene that just happened as he began to assemble his paints and his canvas. But before brush even touched the surface, a smile broke over him and he dismissed the images. And all he could think as the sound of brushstrokes took over the roof was,

*”I don’t need a reference for this one.”*
